segments.s488-g25: پذیره شدش is a causative/passive-like construction: پذیره (reception) + شدن (شد) with the enclitic -ش = 'to him / him', so literally 'reception happened to him' = 'he was received/welcomed'. با introduces the accompanying circumstance; نبرده is the negated perfect participle (ن + برده) meaning 'not taken' and takes سران ('leaders, chiefs') as its object. Grammatically the phrase با نبرده سران modifies how he was received: 'received with the leaders not having been taken.'
segments.s488-g26: A stacked nominal phrase giving the people involved: the adjective/epithet دلاور precedes the plural noun سواران, and the coordinating noun نیزه‌وران follows, listing a second class of fighters. The sequence is a simple apposition/coordination: 'valiant horsemen, (and) spearmen.'
segments.s488-g27: Subject noun سواری ('a rider/horseman') is followed by the past verb بیامد ('came'). هم here emphasizes 'also' or 'too.' اندر زمان is an adverbial prepositional phrase ('in/at the time') giving the temporal setting. Word order (noun + verb + adverbial) is typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s488-g28: A sequence of locative phrases: ز مکه = 'from Mecca' (ز = poetic 'from'); به نزد = 'to the presence of / toward' (به + نزد used together to mark movement toward someone); سکندر is the addressee/name; دمان is a locative noun 'hem/skirts' used here with sکندر to mean 'the skirts/outer parts near Alexander' (poetic for 'near Alexander' or 'to Alexander's camp'). Together they specify origin and destination.
segments.s488-g29: The first که introduces/explains: 'this renown/name (این نامداری)'; the second که begins a relative clause 'who came from the road' (آمد ز راه). The repetition of که is a common classical/poetic device: the first flags the noun and the second introduces the relative clause. ز راه = 'from the way/road' (i.e., 'by the road').
segments.s488-g30: نجوید here is negative-present classical form (ن + جوید) meaning 'does not seek' (the initial ن negates the verbal root). همی is the continuous/emphatic adverb 'continually/indeed.' The direct objects are listed in a series joined by و: تاج (crown/sovereignty), گنج (treasure), و سپاه (and an army). The verb governs the whole coordinated noun list.
segments.s488-g31: نبیره سماعیل = 'descendant/offspring of Ishmael' (نبیره as a genitive/possessive noun phrase). نیک is an adjective 'good/auspicious' modifying اخترست, where اختر ('star') takes the copula -ست meaning 'is a star' or 'is star-like.' The order places the lineage first then the laudatory predicate.
segments.s488-g32: که introduces a clause explaining/justifying the previous statement: پور براهیم = 'son/descendant of Abraham' (پور + proper name in genitive sense), and پیغمبرست is a nominal predicate 'is a prophet' (پیغمبر + copula -ست). The clause states lineage and status in a compact classical form.
segments.s488-g41: بدو گفت is an inverted/order-preserving clause meaning “he said to him” (بدو = به + او, “to him”), with گفت functioning as the reporting verb. نصر‌ای جهاندار شاه is a vocative phrase addressing someone: نصر‌ای (proper name or epithet) followed by two epithets in apposition, جهاندار (world-holder/sovereign) and شاه (king). The vocative marker ای is attached to نصر to call the addressee.
segments.s488-g42: خزاعست is the predicate (خزاع + است) meaning “is Khuzāʿ.” مهتر بدین جایگاه is a nominal phrase giving the predicate complement: مهتر = “chief/headman,” and بدین جایگاه = “in/at this place” (بدین = به + این, an archaising prepositional phrase). Word order is Subject (implied or contextual) omitted, then predicate 'is X' + complement.
segments.s488-g43: چون introduces a temporal clause here meaning “when.” سماعیل is the subject name. زین جهان درگذشت is a verbal phrase meaning “passed away from this world” (زین = از + این in older/poetic spelling; درگذشت is the past tense verb “passed away/died”). Word order is subject + temporal marker + prepositional phrase + verb.
segments.s488-g44: جهانگیر قحطان is a compound personal name/title in apposition (Jahangir Qahtan). بیامد is the simple past verb “came.” ز دشت is a poetic short prepositional phrase meaning “from the plain” (ز = from, دشت = plain). The clause is straightforward: Name + past verb + origin.
segments.s488-g45: This is an instrumental/qualifying prepositional phrase: (ا)با لشکر گشن شمشیرزن = “with an army of hungry swordsmen.” Here ابا functions as an archaic/poetic form of the preposition ‘با’ (“with”); لشکر = “army,” گشن = “hungry,” and شمشیرزن = “swordsman(s).” Adjectival modifiers follow the noun they modify (لشکر گشن شمشیرزن = army [of] hungry swordsmen).
segments.s488-g46: به بیداد بگرفت is the verb phrase ‘took/seized with cruelty’: به here marks manner/instrument (“by/with”), بیداد = “oppression/cruelty,” and بگرفت is the simple past transitive verb “seized/took.” شهر یمن is the direct object “the city of Yemen” (شهر = city, یمن = Yemen). Word order: manner phrase + verb + object.
segments.s488-g47: بسی مردم بیگنه کشته شد is a passive clause: بسی مردم = “many people,” بیگنه qualifies them as “foreign/other” (strangers/outsiders), کشته شد = “were killed” (کشته = past participle ‘killed’, شد as passive auxiliary). Word order places the quantified noun phrase first, then qualifier, then the passive verb.
segments.s488-g48: بدین دودمان روز برگشته شد is another verbal clause with passive/resultative meaning: بدین (= به + این) “by/with this,” دودمان = “dynasty/lineage” (agent/instrument), روز = “the day/fortune/time,” برگشته = past participle “turned back/reversed,” and شد = auxiliary “became/was.” The whole reads “by this dynasty the day/fortune was reversed.”
segments.s488-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. پیش آمدش is a verb phrase where پیش 'in front / before / toward' + آمدش 'came to him' (آمد + enclitic object -ش = 'to him'); word order is verb-final typical of Persian but here the verb آمد is before the pronoun-clitic. نصر is the proper name 'Nasr' (subject). بنواختش is a past/singular perfective verb (بنواخت = he struck/struck at / treated; the final -ش is the 3rd-person object clitic 'him/it'), so the clause reads 'when Nasr came forward, he struck him' with the clitic linking the action to its object.
segments.s488-g34: یکی here functions as an indefinite numeral/article 'one / a certain' modifying جایگه; مایه‌ور is an adjective 'wealthy/rich', and جایگه 'place/seat' is the noun. ساختش is the past tense verb ساخت ('made' or 'built') with the object clitic -ش 'him/it' again; the clause therefore says '(he) made him a wealthy place/seat.' Note the repeated object clitic -ش refers back to the same person previously affected.
segments.s488-g35: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to/unto him' (بدو = به + او) marking the target of the emotion; شاد شد is a two-word predicate meaning 'became happy' (شاد 'happy' + شد 'became' as the past auxiliary); نصر is the subject 'Nasr'. و links to the next clause; گوهر is a proper name here (Gohar) and بگفت is the past reporting verb 'he said' (ب- prefix + گفت).
segments.s488-g36: همه رازها is the noun phrase 'all the secrets' (همه = all; رازها = secrets, plural). برگشاد is a compound past participle/verb form meaning 'were opened/revealed' (بر- prefix + گشاد 'opened'); از نهفت is a prepositional phrase 'from the hidden' (از = from/out of; نهفت = hidden/that which was concealed). The phrase states the result: 'all the secrets were opened from concealment.'
segments.s488-g37: سکندر is the proper name 'Alexander' (subject). چنین داد 'gave thus' = 'gave such' or 'thus gave' with داد as the past of دادن 'to give'; پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply'. بدوی is an attributive/adverbial here meaning 'original/so' or 'thus' (archaic/poetic usage) qualifying the reply. Word order follows classical Persian: Subject – adverbial – verb – object.
segments.s488-g38: که‌ای here introduces reported speech and the vocative: که + ای (classical/pronouncing particle) functions like 'O' before the addressee. پاک‌دل is a compound adjective 'pure‑hearted' (پاک = pure, دل = heart), مهتر is a title 'chief/elder', and راست‌گوی is a compound adjective 'truth‑telling / honest'. Together they form a direct address: 'O pure‑hearted chief, truthful speaker.'
segments.s488-g39: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase 'به + این' meaning 'with/among this' (here written as separate parts in the word list: ب + دین/dوده). دوده is the noun 'smoke/soot' used figuratively for 'throng/party' or 'these people'; اکنون 'now' locates the time; کدامست is the interrogative 'which is it?' (کدام + است fused) and مه is 'moon' (مه = moon) used metaphorically for the most radiant person. The clause asks 'Among these, who now is the moon?' Note that spacing in the provided tokens reflects orthographic splitting; grammatically بدین is a single prepositional phrase.
segments.s488-g40: جز از تو is the exclusion 'except from you' (جز = except; از = from; تو = you). پسندیده is an epithet/name 'Pasandideh' (literally 'the pleasing/approved one') and روزبه is a proper name 'Rouzbeh'; the coordination with و lists them as the ones fitting the role questioned before. The implied copula (is/are) is omitted as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s488-g65: نژاد سماعیل is a definite noun phrase 'the lineage/race of Ishmael'; را is the direct‑object marker putting focus on that noun, and برکشید is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular transitive verb 'raised up' (poetic SOV inversion: object precedes the verb).
segments.s488-g66: هرانکس که begins a relative clause 'whoever/whosoever that…'; او is the 3rd‑person pronoun functioning as the clause's subject; مهتری را uses را to mark the theme 'preeminence' and سزید is the past form of 'سزایدن' meaning 'to befit / to deserve' — together: 'whoever it was that deserved that preeminence.'
segments.s488-g67: پیاده is an adverb 'on foot' modifying درآمد, here a past‑tense verb 'entered/came in'; به introduces the goal with بیت‌الحرام 'into the Kaʿba/sacred precinct' (classical Persian uses به + place for motion into).
segments.s488-g68: سماعیلیان is the plural subject 'the Ishmaelites/descendants of Ishmael'; ز (poetic) here means 'from/because of' and combines with the following شد(e) to form 'became as a result'; شده is the past participle/auxiliary 'became' and شادکام is the resulting predicate adjective 'joyful/fortunate.'
segments.s488-g69: بهر پی is a classical phrase 'for the purpose/of the pursuit' (بهر = 'for', پی = 'following/after, in pursuit'); که introduces the subordinate clause whose verb is برداشت 'took (up)' in past tense; قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar', and ز راه means 'onto/from the road' or simply 'on the road' (poetic use of ز + noun).
segments.s488-g70: همی is a classical adverb placed before a verb to mark continuous or habitual action ('was/kept on'); ریخت is the past‑tense of ریختن meaning 'poured/scattered'; دینار is the direct object 'dinars', and گنجور شاه functions as 'the king's treasury' (poetic compression of possessive relationship: 'from the king's treasury').
segments.s488-g17: خدای جهان را is a topicalized noun phrase: 'the God of the world' with را used here as a topic/emphasis marker (not a strict object marker). نباشد is the negative form of باشد (present/subjunctive/optative in classical/poetic Persian) placed before the noun نیاز; together they mean 'has no need' or 'there is no need (for)'. The order is inverted for poetic effect (topic + negated verb + noun).
segments.s488-g18: نه جای sets up a negated list: نه (not/no) + جای (place/room for), i.e. 'no place for...'. The following nouns خور، کام، آرام، ناز are coordinated by و and enumerate what there is no place for (food/eating, desire, rest/calm, and pampering/affection). The particle نه negates the whole series.
segments.s488-g19: پرستشگهی بود is 'there was a place of worship' (پرستشگه = shrine/temple + بود 'was'). تا بود جای is an idiomatic classical sequence 'as long as there was place/room' (تا = 'as long as/until', بود = past 'was', جای = 'place/room'). The repetition of بود is a poetic, emphatic dating: 'there was ... as long as there was ...'.
segments.s488-g20: بدو اندرون is a locative/dative phrase 'in/within it' (بدو = 'to/at it', اندرون = 'inside'). یاد کرد is a past tense verb phrase 'remembered' (یاد = remembrance; کرد = did/made = 'remembered'); خدای at the end is the object being remembered. Word order here is topical/poetic: locative + verb + object.
segments.s488-g21: پس آمد is a simple past narrative 'then came', with the verb preceding the proper name for poetic rhythm; سکندر is the subject. سوی قادسی is a prepositional phrase 'toward the sacred (place)' (سوی = toward, قادسی = 'sacred/of the sanctuary').
segments.s488-g22: جهانگیر here is an epithet/title applied to Sکندر ('the world-conqueror' / a name/title in apposition). تا جهرم پارسی is an adverbial phrase 'as far as Jahrom in Persia' (تا = 'as far as', جهرم = city name, پارسی = 'Persian/of Persia'). The sequence names the reach/extent of his coming.
segments.s488-g23: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. آگاهی آمد is 'news/information came' (آگاهی = information/news; آمد = came). به نصر قتیب marks the person to whom the news came with به + proper name (نصر قتیب = Nasr Qutayb).
segments.s488-g24: کزو is a relative/adverbial 'from whom' or 'by whom' linking back to the preceding name; بود is the past copula 'was'. مر is the archaic poetic form 'to me' (dative of من). مکه را فر و زیب literally 'Mecca's glory and beauty' uses را as object marker for مکه, then فر = 'royal glory/splendour' and زیب = 'beauty/adornment' coordinated by و. The clause says that by/through him Mecca's splendour and beauty were (for) me.
segments.s488-g9: This is a circumstantial phrase describing manner: ابا ناله بوق و با کوس — literally ‘with the lament of horn and with drum-beating’. ناله and بوق are concrete nouns joined by و; the second با repeats the instrumental preposition to mark کوس. ابا here appears as a poetical/archaic particle introducing the clause and functions adverbially (see senses note).
segments.s488-g10: This is the main directional clause: به خان براهیم آزر برفت = ‘he went to the khan (inn/lord) Ibrahim Azar’. به marks direction/toward, خان is the destination/title, براهیم آزر is the name (appositive), and برفت is the past verb ‘went’ (compound form br+raft in older spelling).
segments.s488-g11: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause (‘for/that’). خان is the subject; حرم را uses the definite object marker را (here marking the thing acted on), and برآورده بود is a past perfect/paraphrastic form (‘had raised/established/provided’) with بود as the past auxiliary.
segments.s488-g12: بدو = ‘to him/for him’ (dative), اندرون = ‘inner’ or ‘inward quarters’, رنجها is the plural ‘sufferings/hardships’, and برده بود is the compound past meaning ‘had carried/taken (in)’ — برده is the participial stem and بود is the auxiliary marking the past perfect sense.
segments.s488-g13: خداوند is the subject ‘God’. خواندش is خواند + the enclitic ـش (him) and here means ‘called/summoned him’. بیت‌الحرام is the object/destination ‘the Kaʿba’ (the sacred house).
segments.s488-g14: بدو = ‘for him/unto him’; شد is the past of شدن ‘became’; همه راه یزدان = ‘the whole way/road of God’; تمام = ‘complete/whole’. Together: ‘for him the entire way to God became complete.’
segments.s488-g15: ز (poetic form of از) marks cause/reason ‘because of’. پاکی = ‘purity’. ورا = ‘to him/unto him’ (variant of به + او). خانه خویش is ‘his own house’ (خوی + ش forming خویش = own/his), and خواند here means ‘called/summoned’ (or ‘brought/received’) — a transitive past verb.
segments.s488-g16: نیایش = ‘prayer’. بران is an adverbial/locative particle ‘thereon/thence’ (meaning ‘there’ in context). کو is a relative/introducing particle ‘that/which’. ت is the 2nd‑person clitic ‘-you’ (here marking the person referenced), را is the object/topic marker, پیش = ‘before/in the presence of’, and خواند here means ‘he recited/read’ (i.e., performed the prayer). The clause therefore means ‘there he recited the prayer which he had recited before you.’
segments.s488-g57: This is a compact verbal phrase where جهانی ("the world") is followed by the participle گرفته ("seized/held"). The prepositional phrase به مشت اندرون (lit. "to/into the fist, inside") states the instrument/place: به + مشت ("fist") with اندرون reinforcing "within/inside." The word order (noun + participle + prepositional phrase) is typical of poetic Persian and omits any overt subject pronoun.
segments.s488-g58: نژاد سماعیل is a genitive noun phrase "the lineage/seed of Isma'il." ازو is an archaic, fused form of از او ("from/of him"), marking source or agency. پر functions as a predicative adjective meaning "full (of)," followed by the poetic preposition ز ("of/from") plus خون ("blood"). Put together: "the lineage of Isma'il was full of blood from him."
segments.s488-g59: سکندر is the subject (Alexander). ز نصر is a source phrase meaning "from Nasr" (here نصر is treated as a proper name). این سخنها ("these words") is the definite/plural object (سخن + ها) and شنید is the simple past 3rd-person verb "he heard." Word order is subject – source phrase – object – verb, common in Persian narrative poetry.
segments.s488-g60: ز تخم خزاعه is a prepositional/genitive phrase "from the seed/offspring of Khazaʿah" (تخم + tribal name خزاعه). هرانکس که is an indefinite relative construction meaning "whoever/whomsoever," with که introducing the relative clause, and دید is the past verb "he saw/observed." Together it yields "from that stock, whomever he saw."
segments.s488-g61: به تن کودکان را is a locative/affected-phrase "in the bodies of the children," with را marking the affected noun. نماندش is negative past (نماند) plus the enclitic -ش (referring to the thing that did not remain, here روان); thus the clause means "no soul remained." روان ("soul/spirit") is the item that is said not to remain, though its grammatical position is after the verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s488-g62: نماندند is the past plural verbal form "they did not remain/there remained not," here used impersonally for people of the stock. زان تخمه کس is a compact phrase "of that seed/offspring no one" (زان = از آن, تخمه = offspring/seed, کس = anyone/person). در جهان adds the location "in the world." The whole clause: "no one of that stock remained in the world."
segments.s488-g63: ز بیداد uses ز in the causal/instrumental sense "by/through (oppression)." بستد is the past 3rd-person verb "he closed/shut/bound." حجاز و یمن are the coordinated objects "Hijaz and Yemen." The clause thus states that, "by oppression, he shut/secured Hijaz and Yemen."
segments.s488-g64: This is a pair of instrumental prepositional phrases: به رای ("by/with counsel/plan") و به مردان شمشیرزن ("and by men who are sword‑wielders"). به expresses instrumentality, رای here means "counsel/plan," and شمشیرزن post-modifies مردان as an adjectival compound "sword‑men/men with swords." The repetition of به is rhetorical/poetic emphasis.
segments.s488-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; لشکر is the subject 'the host/army'; شد is the past verb 'became'; از + خواسته is a prepositional phrase 'free from want' (from desire/need); بی‌نیاز is the predicative adjective 'untroubled/self-sufficient'. Word order is poetic: conjunction + subject + verb + complement.
segments.s488-g2: برو here is an archaic/poetic connective meaning roughly 'then/shortly' (not the literal imperative); ناگذشته modifies زمانی دراز and literally means 'not having passed' so the phrase ناگذشته زمانی دراز = 'not a long time having passed' i.e. 'before long' or 'shortly after'. The phrase should be read as a temporal adverbial.
segments.s488-g3: به شبگیر is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at dusk/nightfall'; برخاست is the past verb 'arose' (subject follows the verb, a common poetic inversion); آوای کوس is a genitive construction 'the sound/voice of the drum'.
segments.s488-g4: هوا شد = 'the air/mood became' (شد as copula/become). به کردار = 'in the manner/likeness of' (prepositional phrase), and چشم خروس is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the rooster's eye' used metaphorically. Word order is subject (هوا) + verb (شد) + simile phrase.
segments.s488-g5: ز بس is a causal/partitive opening meaning 'from/owing to so many' (ز + بس); the following list نیزه و پرنیانی names the items ('spears and silken pennons'), and درفش is the head noun 'the banner/standard' affected by that abundance.
segments.s488-g6: ستاره شده is a passive/perfect-like construction 'the star became/was made' (شده = become/made); the colors سرخ و زرد و بنفش are a coordinated series of complements describing the resulting appearance. The verb links the banner's star to these color predicates.
segments.s488-g7: سکندر is the subject 'Alexander'; بیامد is the past verb 'came'; به سوی حرم is a direction phrase 'toward the sanctuary/harem' (به سوی = 'toward', حرم = 'sanctuary/forbidden place' depending on context).
segments.s488-g8: گروهی ازو = 'a group of/ some of them' (گروهی used distributively); شاد = 'joyful/happy'; و = 'and'; بهری here is an archaic/distributive word meaning 'the others' (used to set up 'some ... and others'), and دژم is the adjective 'sullen/grim'. The construction contrasts portions of the company.
segments.s488-g49: نیامد is a past negative predicate used idiomatically here meaning 'did not come to be pleasing' (i.e., 'did not approve'). The phrase places the addressee-marked noun جهان‌آفرین را ('to/for the world‑creator') immediately after the verb, a poetic word order: literally 'did not come — to the world‑creator — approval'. را is the definite/object marker attached to جهان‌آفرین; پسند is a noun meaning 'approval/liking' and is the thing that 'did not come' to the Creator.
segments.s488-g50: برو functions as a short discourse particle meaning 'thereupon/then' in this poetic context (not the literal imperative 'go'); since that sense is not listed in the provided options, it is treated as unmatched. تیره شد is a past verbal phrase 'became dark/ominous' (تیره = dark/ominous, شد = 'became'). رای چرخ بلند is the agent/subject phrase 'the counsel/decision of the lofty wheel' (رای = counsel/plan/decision; چرخ بلند = the high/lofty wheel — i.e. the heavens/fate). Word order places the time/particle first, the predicate next, and the subject phrase after it, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s488-g51: خزاعه is a proper name (the Khaza'ah tribe) and functions as the subject. بیامد is simple past 'came'. چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. او گشت خاک is a subordinate clause 'he became dust' (او = 'he'; گشت is past of گشتن 'became/turned'; خاک = 'dust/ashes'), a poetic way to say he died or was reduced to dust.
segments.s488-g52: بر introduces the target of the farewell (here translated idiomatically as 'to/against' or 'with regard to'); رنج و بیداد are the coordinated nouns 'suffering and injustice/oppression'. بدرود is 'farewell' and functions here as the action (they 'bade farewell' to those things); پاک follows and operates adverbially ('cleanly/utterly/altogether') modifying the farewell. The whole phrase means they were rid of or parted with suffering and oppression completely.
segments.s488-g53: حرم تا یمن is a span 'from the sanctuary to Yemen' where تا is 'as far as'. پاک here qualifies the whole span ('wholly' / 'entirely'). بر دست اوست is the copular clause 'is in his hand' (بر دست = 'upon the hand', i.e. 'under his power'; اوست = 'it is his'). The adjective/order places the domain first and then states it is under his control.
segments.s488-g54: به introduces direction/limit 'to/into'; دریای مصر is a genitive structure 'the Sea of Egypt'. اندرون modifies it as 'inner' or 'inward parts'. شست اوست is a slightly idiomatic locution here: شست (literally 'thumb' or a part of the hand) is used metonymically for 'reach/grip' and اوست is the copula 'is his' — together meaning 'his reach/hold extends into' the inner parts of the Egyptian sea. The clause therefore says his power extends even into the inner waters of Egypt's sea.
segments.s488-g55: سر از راه پیچیده is an idiom meaning 'he turned his head away from the way' (سر = 'head' used figuratively; از راه = 'from the path'; پیچیده is a past/adjectival form of پیچیدن 'to turn', 'to veer'). و links the clauses. داد نه here uses داد as the verbal root 'give' (rather than the abstract noun 'justice') followed by the postpositive negator نه to mean 'and did not give' — understood in context as 'did not give justice' or 'did not act justly'; the object (justice) is implied by the context.
segments.s488-g56: ز یزدان indicates source/relation 'from/of God'. یکی را is an indefinite object 'one' (یکی) with the object marker را. به دل is a locative phrase 'into the heart' (به here expressing direction/placement). یاد نه uses یاد 'remembrance/memory' in a verb‑like sense plus the negator نه to mean 'did not remember' or 'kept no remembrance' — so the whole clause says he had no remembrance of God in his heart.

sentences.s488-s13: He was received (welcomed), with the leaders not taken, (by) valiant horsemen and spearmen.
sentences.s488-s14: A rider also came, at that time, from Mecca to the presence/skirts of Alexander.
sentences.s488-s15: For this famous one who came by the road does not seek crown, treasure, or an army.
sentences.s488-s16: He is a descendant of Ishmael, a good/auspicious star, for he is a son of Abraham and a prophet.
sentences.s488-s21: He said to him, “O Nasr, sovereign king, Khuzāʿ is the chief of this place.”
sentences.s488-s22: When Isma'il passed from this world, Jahangir Qahtan came from the plain.
sentences.s488-s23: With an army of hungry swordsmen he seized the city of Yemen with cruelty.
sentences.s488-s24: Many strangers were slain; by this dynasty the day (i.e. the fortunes of the time) was turned back.
sentences.s488-s17: When Nasr came forward, he struck him and made for him a rich seat.
sentences.s488-s18: Nasr became glad at him, and Gohar said: all the secrets were revealed from the hidden.
sentences.s488-s19: Alexander gave this answer and replied: 'O pure‑hearted chief, truthful speaker...'
sentences.s488-s20: Among this throng now, which one is the moon? Except for you, (it is) Pasandideh and Rouzbeh.
sentences.s488-s33: He exalted the lineage of Ishmael — whoever was worthy of that preeminence.
sentences.s488-s34: He went on foot into the Kaʿba; the Ishmaelites from it became joyful.
sentences.s488-s35: Because the Caesar took to the road in pursuit, the king's treasury poured out dinars.
sentences.s488-s9: The God of the world has no need — there is no place for eating, desire, rest, or pampering.
sentences.s488-s10: There was a place of worship so long as there was room; within it God was remembered.
sentences.s488-s11: Then Alexander came toward the sacred place; the world-conqueror went as far as Jahrom in Persia.
sentences.s488-s12: When news reached Nasr Qutayb, from whom I had (or received) Mecca's glory and beauty.
sentences.s488-s5: With the lamenting blast of horns and the beating of drums, (Taft) went to the khan of Ibrahim Azar.
sentences.s488-s6: For the khan had established the harem for him and had brought inward sufferings to him.
sentences.s488-s7: God summoned him to the Kaʿba; for him the whole way to God became complete.
sentences.s488-s8: Because of his purity he called him to his own house; there he recited the prayer that he had recited before you.
sentences.s488-s29: He had gripped the world within his fist; the line of Isma'il was filled with blood because of him.
sentences.s488-s30: Alexander heard these words from Nasr; from the seed of Khazaʿah he saw whoever (there was).
sentences.s488-s31: No soul remained in the bodies of the children; no one of that stock was left in the world.
sentences.s488-s32: By oppression he closed off Hijaz and Yemen; by counsel and by sword-wielding men.
sentences.s488-s1: When the host had become free from want, then, before a long time had passed...
sentences.s488-s2: At dusk the sound of the war-drum rose; the air became like the eye of a rooster.
sentences.s488-s3: Because of so many spears and silken pennons, the banner's star became red and yellow and purple.
sentences.s488-s4: Alexander came toward the sanctuary; some of his company were joyful and others sullen.
sentences.s488-s25: The world's Creator did not approve; thereupon the lofty wheel's counsel turned dark.
sentences.s488-s26: The Khaza'ah came when he turned to dust; they bade a clean farewell to suffering and oppression.
sentences.s488-s27: From the sanctuary as far as Yemen, all is wholly in his hand; his reach extends into the inner waters of the Sea of Egypt.
sentences.s488-s28: He turned his head away from the path and did not give justice; he held no remembrance of God in his heart.
